Brian Chesky speaks out. This is the first time he is speaking out in detail after “Founder Mode” became phrase de jour few weeks ago, along with backlash to it, following the now famous Paul Graham… | Rafat Ali | 33 comments
Rafat Ali
1y
Brian Chesky speaks out. This is the first time he is speaking out in detail after “Founder Mode” became phrase de jour few weeks ago, along with backlash to it, following the now famous Paul Graham essay. Below is a clip of my interview with him at Skift Global Forum 2024, earlier this week. Also his views on AI. Worth a listen. "Great leaders are in the details. That's what Founder Mode is...I partner with all the team members to make the decisions. And btw, if you are not in details, your leaders are not in details. I believe my experience, whether you are Founder not, applies: be in the details."

First of all, thank you for having me here remotely and just I'm the design studios behind me. So like behind me is where you'll see some of our designers and this space was actually designed by Johnny. I've so fun fact so. Counter mode. Let me just give you a tiny bit of back story. About a month ago, Paul Graham asked me and Gary Can, who's president of White Hunter asked me to do a talk and he said this talks off the record. Obviously 2 hour story in a minute. But the short answer is. You know, like we just heard from The Secret before the pandemic rocked our world. We lost 80% of our business in eight weeks in. Great leaders hire great people and empower them to do their job. I think that's terrible leadership. And I don't think Steve Jobs would have ever believed that. Walt Disney didn't believe that. The, the, it's the plumbing that will be changed first before the veneer gets changed. This is ridiculous and full of wild assumptions. How does he know how 'every other CEO operates'? "If you're not in the details, your leaders are not in the details". That's just not true. It's possible to ask searching questions and learn if your team are in the details. But you cannot be in the weeds of every single decision without creating a bottle neck. And at what point is the decision allowed v not allowed without CEO sign off? Does he decide what brand toilet paper goes in the bathrooms? Spin the web, but don't catch the fly. Meaning you create an environment for autonomous decision-making by people you know you can trust. It's the only way to multiply impact. Any other way is inefficient.

Rafat Ali great interview, thought provoking and definitely insightful. “Founder Mode” definitely challenges the recent mind set of how decisions should be made in an organization and potentially questions how employee empowerment would be thought about. I have heard some founders say “I will not be making any decisions” implying I will empower my team. That point of view is definitely being challenged by Brian. For me the “being in the details” does not equate to “I decide what toilet paper goes in the bathroom ( as someone above made a reference). It’s about knowing your product, understanding the details of why one idea is better than the other for the customers, or future growth, etc. if you are not in the details, how would you know?

I think that it's really important that when you pick partners, there are people that you could be friends with. They're people you can imagine spending more time than your spouse. You're gonna probably spend more time your cofounder than your partner, actually. And there are people that are so good, they almost intimidate you, they challenge you. But there are also complementary. One time, like a decade ago, we each did like some kind of personality test. I don't, it wasn't like in any agreement, but it was like one of those kinds of things. And there's like, you know, the, the personality tests around like a wheel, right? And they, they kind of put you in a wheel. And it turned out that when they plotted our three personalities, they formed a perfect equilateral triangle. It was like and and and and it made so much sense. That it's about the sense of mutual respect. People that are so good they almost intimidate you, but they're so complimentary. And there was such a deep respect. And we had a rule, and the rule was that that winning an argument was never more important than preserving the relationship. And the reason that's important is because if you start a company, you're gonna have to debate like 100,000 things. At least 10,000, right? You're probably debating like three things a day. And so it's like 1000 things a year, 10 years, that's 10, 000 things. So no one argument can be the thing it has. There has to be this larger sense that we're a ban. And I think you got to really work hard at the relationship, almost like exercising. If you don't keep exercising, you lose, you get out of shape. And so we worked really, really hard. And one of the things we did in 2009 is we said every single Sunday we're going to meet, no matter how busy we get. Now it's like sometimes Sunday becomes Thursday, but we basically meet every single week. And to this day, we still do those calls. And I think a lot of human connection requires constant contact, constant connection, a deep sense of respect, a sense that like, you know, I'm only here because of them, a sense of humility and gratitude and a sense that I'm never going to try to win because if I win alone, I'm not going very far.

We've seen this time and time again. Founders start out with great good will, focus and commitment. And then something changes. Life gets in the way. And suddenly the founders find themselves drifting (or driving) in different directions.
One great approach is to sit down in the early days and draw up a founders' charter. Consider things like:
\* What are you "for" - where do your goals meet your values
\* What do you own and what do you support - what are your founder "swim lanes" and where do each of you take the lead and where do you support the other?
\* How you fix a hole - when business get hard how will you work together to dig yourself out of a hole?
\* How do you part - what does a change or consolidation of ownership look like when conditions change? What else should founders consider?[#entrepreneurs](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/entrepreneurs&trk=public_post-text)[#startups](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/startups&trk=public_post-text)

"A Priceless Lesson for Founders and Teams"
This advice from Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky deeply resonates with us at OBENTaS GLOBAL. Just like Brian mentioned, winning an argument is never more important than preserving the relationship. At OBENTaS, we’ve embraced a similar principle—it’s not about titles; it’s about what makes the most sense for our mission, vision, and culture.
Every day, we engage in countless debates—not to prove who’s right but to find the best solution. Our decisions are driven by who makes the stronger case and what aligns with our collective goals. It’s this level of trust, connection, and shared purpose that keeps our team strong and unified.
This is how startups not only survive but thrive. 3moEdited
This is 100% true. Co-founder misalignment and lack of chemistry are among the top reasons startups fail before they even take off. It’s a hard truth many founders (and investors) have witnessed firsthand.
On this journey, I’ve met at least four companies where the co-founders are spouses or life partners—and it’s working. They seem to thrive together, and it’s made me wonder: is this a new trend?
When you think about it, it makes sense. Partners already spend a lot of time together, and conversations naturally flow. If you’re brainstorming about family or household challenges, why not expand that dialogue to include business ideas—especially if you have complementary skills?
Thoughts? I am also aware VCs and investors don’t like this too much.

Is Winning Really Worth It? 💭Airbnb CEO[Brian Chesky](https://www.linkedin.com/in/brianchesky?trk=public_post-text)nails it with this insight: “Winning an argument was never more important than preserving the relationship.”
Let that sink in. How often do we let our egos take the wheel, sacrificing connection and trust for the fleeting satisfaction of “being right”?
In business - and life - success isn’t about proving a point; it’s about building a foundation strong enough to weather 100,000 debates. This advice doesn’t just apply to startups; it applies to every relationship, every partnership, and every team dynamic.
Misalignment isn’t just a startup killer - it’s a momentum killer. Without trust and shared purpose, even the best ideas fall apart.
So, here’s the challenge:
•Are you fighting to win or fighting for alignment?
•How can you shift from ego-driven debates to mission-driven collaboration?
What do you think matters more - the argument or the relationship?[#Leadership](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/leadership&trk=public_post-text)[#TeamAlignment](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/teamalignment&trk=public_post-text)[#CourageousLeadership](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/courageousleadership&trk=public_post-text)[#Startups](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/startups&trk=public_post-text)[#TrustMatters](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/trustmatters&trk=public_post-text)[#WinningTogether](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/winningtogether&trk=public_post-text)[John Dare](https://au.linkedin.com/in/john-dare-9006252?trk=public_post-text)[Scott Dare](https://www.linkedin.com/in/scott-dare?trk=public_post-text)[Jen W.](https://au.linkedin.com/in/jen-white-coach?trk=public_post-text)[Emotous](https://au.linkedin.com/company/emotous?trk=public_post-text)

Brian Chesky's (Airbnb) famous Founder Mode talk argues almost all "CEO advice" is wrong, example: "The role of a great leader is to hire great people and empower them to do their job"—he says if you follow that and are not careful, your company will be destroyed. Brain says Founder Mode's single principle is "To be in the details" and that great leadership is presence not absence. In this clip, Brain explains what happened to Airbnb—the highlights: 1/ Airbnb was a matrix org with many different teams. Teams created teams that created sub-teams that created more sub-teams. 5 teams turned into 20 teams and now dependent teams were lined up like a deli waiting multi-months and thus they just hired and created their own teams. Each division has a General Manager (GM) and each GM wanted to create their own mini-GMs. >> This created a lot of bureaucracy and meetings about meetings. Resulting in advocacy for resources. Creating politics. >> Then, because it's hard to track what everyone is doing, you get a free-for-all. Resulting in lack of accountability. Creating complacency. This makes it indistinguishable between the good and bad people. The good people move on saying the company changed and is slow. 2/ The only thing that binded the company together were metrics and strategic priorities. No cohesive roadmap. >> The biggest problem is the CEO is separated from the product. 3/ A "manager" who does not know how to do the job they are managing is like a calvary general who cannot ride a horse. >> Brian realized they had 50 year olds managing 40 year olds managing 30 year olds managing the interns doing the job. Too many layers, no value. >> "B Players" hire A LOT of C Players because they like empire building. 4/ Brian used the COVID crisis as an opportunity to rebuild the company. What he did: >> Changed from a divisional structure to a functional organization. >> As few employees as possible (less people = less meetings). Be the Navy Seals, not the Navy. >> Removed layers of management and went to functional leaders (you must be able to do the work first). You don't manage people, you manage people through the work. >> Created Product Marketing function which is product management + outbound marketing. >> All product managers either became product marketers or program managers. Thoughts?

Problem with being a CEO is I think. Almost all the advice and everything they teach, like Harvard Business School, I don't say everything, but a lot of it is kind of wrong. Like, for example, the role of a great leader is to hire great people and empower them to do their job. And I'm not saying I disagree with that statement, but that statement, if you do that and you're not careful, your company will be destroyed. So let me tell you how that would happen. We had a company where we were like a matrix organization. And so like we had all these different teams and by the way, there's no governor how many teams there are. So teams can create teams can create sub teams can create sub teams that people can decide how many manager levels they create. Like if you're not careful, people do this. And why do they do this? Because they want to have new teams. They want to have jobs, responsibility, all these scopes. So let's take like a marketing or creative department. There's a team in Airbnb doing graphics and like different, different parts of the site, any graphics, the advertising, these graphics and when it was. Five teams. The five teams would ask the graphics department for graphics and they'd have like 5 requests and then pretty soon it's 20 teams. And once it's 20 teams, they're like the deli, there's a line out the block, there's a multi month wait. And then what happens is that graphics team, the central service kind of like gives up and everything seems pointless and the teams waiting forever give up. And they say give me my own people. So now they get their own graphics team. So now you have 5 or 10 graphics teams and you can do the same thing with technology. And product, ohh, you can have ten data teams that have different metrics and like we can go down the list. So now you have 10 divisions. Now those 10 divisions are now wanting to go in different directions and they have general managers and general managers are like little Russian babushka dolls. They wanna create miniature general managers and miniature miniature general managers. And so now you don't have 10 teams, you've got actually 100 teams because you got these little babushkas running around and. They're going in 100 directions with different technology and though it seems faster, like we can be like a startup, it's a lot easier to spin stuff up. You end up with a lot of bureaucracy. You end up with a company where there's meetings about meetings, where metrics and strategic priorities the only thing that bind the company together. There is no cohesive product road map. Everything is a different time horizon. It's all short term oriented. And the biggest problem of all is the CEO gets separated from their own product, like your core. Asset was you built the product, you. Every CEO, I think with only a few exceptions, should be the chief product officer of the company, because isn't the most important thing of a company to make a product and shouldn't the person knows the most about the product be the CEO? And yet you are told to be separate from your product. And I noticed this thing where there was more bureaucracy. There were these divisions. The divisions. Then they have to advocate for resources. The advocate advocacy creates politics. And then you have a situation where it's hard to track what everyone's doing. So you have this like free for all. There's not a lot of accountability. That leads to complacency. That complacency means that like the bad people and the good people are indistinguishable. So the good people tend to move on. They say the company is. Change the company slows down and one day you wake up. Ohh. And by the way you have leaders that are quote managers. I don't like managers. We don't have a single manager in Airbnb and I put in air quotes. A manager that doesn't know how to do the job they're managing is like a cavalry general. We can't run a ride a horse. And a lot of companies do that. So we only allowed managers that were experts. But for a long time, we had managers. And one day I woke up and I realized I had 50 year olds managing 40 year olds managing 30 year olds managing interns, doing the job with all these layers that weren't adding any value. And I woke up in 2019, ten years in, and it was just kind of soul crushing. I didn't know what to do. I meet two people that changed my life. One was Johnny if he was head of industrial design at Apple. The other was a guy named Hiroki Asai was. Projector Apple and they taught me about how Steve Jobs ran Apple and it was the opposite of everything I described. NOT EVERY BRAND NEEDS TWO CAPTAINS But in some cases... it’s the only way to scale fast without losing focus. Let me explain 👇 When it makes sense 💡 Sometimes you need two brains at the top. One driving the brand, comms, partnerships. The other focused on product, ops, finance. Creative and operations. Both critical. Both needed. Every. Single. Week. This is where Co-CEOs can really work. Netflix does it. Warby Parker too. Not just symbolic — real clarity in who does what. Too much complexity for one person? 🤯 Multi-category. Multi-channel. Multi-region. If you’ve built this kind of machine… one person might slow it down. Split roles like this: - CEO A → Expansion, Construction, HR, Tech, Finance, External relations - CEO B → Product, eCom, Retail, Marketing, Supply Chain Share vision. Divide execution. But it needs to be very clean. No grey zones. Two founders? Can’t choose one? 👥 Seen it before. Two strong founders with different skills. Neither can (or should) step down. It can work with a strong Chair in place. They hold the balance. Break ties. Keep the long-term view intact. When it absolutely does not work 🚫 If your house is on fire? You need one clear voice. One decision-maker. Also doesn’t work when there’s no clarity. Saw this at Salesforce. Didn’t end well. No clear ownership = confusion everywhere. Make it work, or don’t do it at all ✅ If you’re going Co-CEO, it needs real structure: - One voice to the outside world - Clear decision matrix down to the teams - Control functions (CFO, HR, Legal) report to one CEO only - Crisis protocol defined - Formal review in 12–24 months It’s not about “two friends sharing power”. It’s about scaling with clarity.

The way tech companies organize their teams is changing fast. The old model of “general managers” running business units is on its way out. In its place, more companies are moving back to a functional management model — grouping teams by domain like design, engineering, or sales. Block made headlines this year when Jack Dorsey told employees: “We’re going to reorganize our entire company by function and disband our business unit reporting structure.” Airbnb has followed the same path. Brian Chesky explained: “At Airbnb … we have a head of design, a head of engineering, a head of product marketing … we don’t have these little pods.” This shift raises the bar for managers. They can no longer be generalists who know a little about everything. They now need functional expertise in the domain they manage. An engineering manager must know engineering. A design manager must know design. Without that depth, they can’t coach their teams, spot real quality, or make the right calls under pressure. The benefits are significant: • Faster decisions because leaders understand the work firsthand • Higher standards because experts know what high performance looks like • CEOs gain a direct line into each function and stay closer to the details I’m seeing the same trend with our customers. When managers are true domain experts, teams move quicker, quality goes up, and organizations stay more agile. General managers are fading. Domain experts are rising. For fast-growing tech companies, this is becoming the new winning strategy.

When I started IDigitalPreneur, it was just a small team and a dream bigger than our resources. At the same time, I was building my YouTube channel and creating content. I thought I could do everything myself. But I quickly realised that wouldn’t work. I had to lead differently because, for me, my team’s growth mattered as much as the company’s. So I chose to trust them, give them ownership, and let them experiment. At first, I wasn’t sure if it was the right call. But it turned out to be one of the best decisions I ever made because the best ideas often came from the people I empowered, not just from me. I built a habit of putting people first. Every decision started with one question: What’s best for the team? What’s best for the company? That mindset changed how we worked, how we grew, and how I led. Because a company doesn’t grow from revenue alone, it grows from people who believe in the vision and feel trusted to own it. Once I shifted my focus from “How can I do more?” to “How can my team succeed more?” everything changed. Our ideas got sharper, our systems stronger, and our vision bigger than any one of us. Being a CEO isn’t about doing everything yourself or chasing revenue alone. It’s about creating a space where others can shine, and when they do, the company grows in ways you could never imagine alone. What’s one leadership habit that changed the way your team works?

𝑻𝒉𝒆𝒚 𝒕𝒆𝒍𝒍 𝒕𝒉𝒓𝒆𝒆 𝒌𝒊𝒏𝒅𝒔 𝒐𝒇 𝒔𝒕𝒐𝒓𝒊𝒆𝒔 𝒕𝒉𝒂𝒕 𝒈𝒓𝒐𝒘 𝒘𝒊𝒕𝒉 𝒕𝒉𝒆𝒎. 1️⃣ 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐎𝐫𝐢𝐠𝐢𝐧 𝐒𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐲: 𝐰𝐡𝐲 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐛𝐥𝐞𝐦 𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐨 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐛𝐞𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐢𝐭 𝐛𝐞𝐜𝐚𝐦𝐞 𝐚 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭 People don’t buy into startups; they buy into missions that feel personal. Your “why” isn’t a slogan, it’s your credibility. It’s not “We’re solving delivery delays.” It’s “I once waited 4 hours for food and realized how broken convenience could feel.” When your audience sees that the product grew out of a real frustration or experience, they connect emotionally before they convert logically. 2️⃣ 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐜𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐒𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐲: 𝐡𝐨𝐰 𝐲𝐨𝐮’𝐫𝐞 𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠, 𝐢𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐢𝐦𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐯𝐢𝐧𝐠 This is where growth lives. Talk about your experiments, what you tried, what didn’t work, and what changed after. It could be: “We learned that 80% of users drop off after onboarding, so we rebuilt the flow and improved retention by 12%.” Numbers build credibility, but transparency builds community, it turns your journey into shared progress. 3️⃣ 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐏𝐞𝐨𝐩𝐥𝐞 𝐒𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐲: 𝐡𝐨𝐰 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐭𝐞𝐚𝐦 𝐚𝐫𝐞 𝐞𝐯𝐨𝐥𝐯𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐛𝐞𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭 Founders often forget this one. Users don’t just care about your roadmap; they care about your mindset. Talk about what you’re learning as a leader, what your team culture is teaching you, or how you’re navigating burnout, hiring, or uncertainty. Because great marketing isn’t only what the product says. It’s how the people behind it grow. If I had 1000 people, I don't want to put them in 1000 boats. I want to put 1000 people in one boat and I want everyone rowing in One Direction. There's a different way of running a company. It's a it's at least worth trying. It's the way of startup is run #1 stay as small as possible to have as few people as possible. Do as few things as possible. Half you details, perfect every detail. Stay small like a startup. The second thing is be in the details. I think the CEO should be in the details, but CEOs are mostly told not to be in the details. Because that's micromanaging. And if you micromanage people, you disempower them. I actually think the best way to empower somebody is going to be the details. You don't tell them how to do their job, but you know everything they're doing so you can actually help them. But the third and maybe most important principle is everyone rose in the same direction.

This reminded me of 3 things: 1/ I agree. Recurring 1:1s are not needed at the CEO / C-suite level. The direct reports are senior enough executives to discern when an escalation or a private 1:1 is needed. 2/ Brian Chesky mentioned Jensen Huang (Cofounder & CEO of NVIDIA) and that he also does not do 1:1s with his 60 direct reports because (a) he wants a culture where there is no privileged access to information; and (b) he gives people feedback right there in front of everybody because feedback is learning and learning from mistakes, other people's mistakes, is the best way to learn. 3/ Ethan Evans shared that Jeff Bezos felt 1:1s at the executive level were low-value, and he stopped having any 1:1s himself. Jeff felt that 1:1s with very senior employees often turned into an arena for complaining about one another, which was a waste of time. Brian Chesky (Cofounder/CEO of Airbnb) does not believe in 1:1s and said almost all great CEOs don't have them: "Almost every CEO did 1:1s and realized that the 1:1 model was flawed. It's a recurring 1 hour 1-on-1 meeting where the employee owns the agenda and what happens is they often don't talk about things you want to talk about. You become their therapist... Often time they are bringing you problems that you want other people in the room to hear and there are very few times that employees should come to you 1:1 without their people... It will text a lot of employees and I'll call employees if we wanna talk. You know, a meeting is not the best way to do it. I don't do one-on-one meetings really, unless it's like somebody. I don't believe in one on ones and almost no great CEO in history has ever done them. Very few. Like, you know, again, we can, we can go down the list of CEOs who don't do why don't you? Almost every CEO did one on ones and they realized that the one-on-one model is flawed. It's recurring one hour, one-on-one meeting where the employee owns the agenda and what happens? Is like they often don't talk about the things you want to talk about. You become like their therapists, but more you know, they bring you problems, but oftentimes they're bringing you problems that you want other people in the room to hear. And there was there's very few times employees should come to you one-on-one without other people if they're concerned about something, if they're having a difficult time in their personal life, if they want to confide in something they don't feel safe like telling a group, but that should be like infrequent if that's happening frequently, that is a very. Ominous sign for your organization. So Jensen Huang says I don't do one on ones because I want everyone to be part of the solution and get the wisdom. When you make lots of one-on-one decisions, then people feel like they don't have decision made. They don't feel like they have a voice they didn't have buy in. So generally you do group meetings. All of us choose to do group meetings. Now there's a caveat. I do a lot of one-on-one calls and emails and I'll call someone and say, hey, what's the status of this? Like I think blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. But it's not a recurring meeting with them owning the agenda. It's me. When I need something, I'll call somebody. So it's I do meetings, they're recurring meetings, they're group meetings. Everyone hears there's notes taken. The way tech companies organize their teams is changing fast. The old model of “general managers” running business units is on its way out. In its place, more companies are moving back to a functional management model — grouping teams by domain like design, engineering, or sales. Block made headlines this year when Jack Dorsey told employees: “We’re going to reorganize our entire company by function and disband our business unit reporting structure.” Airbnb has followed the same path. Brian Chesky explained: “At Airbnb … we have a head of design, a head of engineering, a head of product marketing … we don’t have these little pods.” This shift raises the bar for managers. They can no longer be generalists who know a little about everything. They now need functional expertise in the domain they manage. An engineering manager must know engineering. A design manager must know design. Without that depth, they can’t coach their teams, spot real quality, or make the right calls under pressure. The benefits are significant: • Faster decisions because leaders understand the work firsthand • Higher standards because experts know what high performance looks like • CEOs gain a direct line into each function and stay closer to the details I’m seeing the same trend with our customers. When managers are true domain experts, teams move quicker, quality goes up, and organizations stay more agile. General managers are fading. Domain experts are rising. For fast-growing tech companies, this is becoming the new winning strategy.

The most expensive mistake we see in high-growth companies isn't overhiring or underinvesting. It's building systems that work perfectly at their current size—and catastrophically at 2X scale. We call it the "Scale Cliff." Some warning signs you're approaching one: ✓ Communication depends on tribal knowledge ✓ Decision workflows rely on 2-3 key people ✓ Your tech stack shows early signs of strain ✓ Quick fixes outnumber strategic solutions ✓ Teams are solving symptoms, not root causes We observed this firsthand when a rapidly growing fintech increased from 50 to 200 employees in 14 months. What broke first? Not their product or customer acquisition. Their knowledge management and decision systems collapsed. Meetings multiplied. Context was lost. New hires took months to become productive. Priorities changed weekly. Their systems weren't designed to distribute decision-making at scale. The irony? The team was brilliant. The product was growing. The market was strong. But their operating system couldn't handle the complexity. Smart leaders recognize that bottlenecks are actually systems problems in disguise. The question isn't "How do we grow faster?" It's "What systems would need to exist for growth to feel effortless?" Systems thinking isn't just for engineers. It's the hidden advantage of every team that's successfully scaled. A.G.A.I.N #23 – A Good Advice I Neglected: “You can’t lead on autopilot.” One of the most common patterns I see with founders is this: they’re everywhere and nowhere at the same time. Juggling investor calls, firefighting operations, hiring, expansion, product. They’re stretched thin. The calendar is full, but the team is still waiting — for clarity, for direction, for a decision. When people don’t get time with their leaders, the gap fills up with confusion. They start guessing priorities, second-guessing each other, and third-guessing themselves. The problem isn’t intent. It’s bandwidth. When founders (or anyone in leadership) are too busy to sit down with their teams, everything slows down — except anxiety. What works isn’t more effort. It’s structured attention. Portion clear time for what actually builds clarity — reviews, check-ins, and one-time projects that set direction or fix systemic issues. Be hands-on where the fundamentals are being built. Don’t delegate the decisions that define direction. There are ways to make limited founder time go further. → Detailed notes and clear summaries: Every project and process update email should call out decisions needed, blockers, and key takeaways. Don’t make people read through threads to find the point. → Escalations that are documented: Use WhatsApp for urgency, but always follow up with an email for continuity and retention. What gets buried in a chat disappears from collective memory. → Broad meetings with the right people: Include cross-functional stakeholders so context travels horizontally, not just top-down. A good meeting saves twenty clarifying ones later. → Be concise: If you’re working with a founder, lead with a TL;DR that captures the issue, the decision required, and key context. Make it easy for them to say yes or no quickly, and then dive into details. You’ll save time, get faster responses, and reduce back-and-forth. People don’t need constant access. They need consistent clarity. You don’t have to be everywhere. You just have to show up when it counts. Because when leaders run on autopilot, everyone else is left circling the runway. PC: IKEA on a toasty October morning in Bangalore. Yes, we are in that part of the year where living in Bangalore makes sense.

I think he's saying he's ALWAYS been involved in the details, but that previously he'd get involved late in the game, reactively, because things were broken. Now, he's involved up front. That's useful strategically (ensuring everyone is operating under a shared set of principles and goals) and tactically (helping projects get off to a good start before there's a lot of investment to unwind). That's both going to be more effective and, as he says, less work. And, to come back to the whole Founder Mode thing, it's completely compatible with giving people autonomy. With that up-front check-in they can go off with confidence and get stuff done their way, while still operating within organizational constraints and guidelines.

Brian Chesky on what your startup can learn from Ernest Shackleton’s famous job ad
At the end of the interview process, the Airbnb founder will usually tell the candidate all of the reasons they shouldn’t take the job:
“It’s going to be the longest hours you’ve ever had. I’m going to hold you to higher standards. It’s going to be day and night. It’s going to be this. It’s going to be that.”
As Brian points out, this is often the opposite of what recruiters will do:
“Recruiters often want to sell their company as a country club - we have great benefits, we have great this, we have great that. And it creates mismanaged expectations.”
But the best people want a challenge.
Brian reads the famous job ad Ernest Shackleton ran in a newspaper to recruit men for his Endurance expedition:
“Men wanted for hazardous journey. Low wages, bitter cold, long hours of complete darkness. Safe return doubtful. Honour and recognition in event of success.”
Brian believes this is one of the best job ads ever:
“Don’t sell a rosy picture of the experience… Why do the most fit people in the world want to be Navy SEALs? Because they want the challenge. The average person doesn’t want a challenge, but the best people want a challenge. And so that will turn off mediocre people, but it will turn on great people.”

We once built a company list for a client that included a CEO’s side hustle nonprofit—because we knew it’d spark a shared-value connection.
That’s not a flex. That’s the level of detail we hit before we even touch a job board.
It starts with research that goes way beyond “who’s hiring.”
We dig into industries, funding rounds, team pages, leadership interviews, podcast guest spots… even the comments under a company’s LinkedIn posts.
Why? Because job searching isn’t just matching keywords.
It’s matching people—what they care about, how they speak, who they’ll say yes to.
So when we build a custom firm list for a client, we’re not pulling from a generic spreadsheet.
We’re looking at the founder’s background, recent team changes, Glassdoor reviews, and niche hiring trends to decide if it’s worth pursuing.
Then we tailor every single application —not just the resume, but the outreach strategy too.
One client had 14 applications out. 7 turned into interviews. 3 became offers.
Compare that to the spray-and-pray method most job seekers use:
50+ apps a week, no follow-up, and wondering why nothing sticks.
It’s not magic. It’s just manual.
And it works because we’re not afraid to slow down.
What would you want someone to research about a company before applying on your behalf?

Product Marketing Lead @Prospeo.io 🟥1moEdited
Here is one way you can stand out from 99% of other applicants.
When I first applied to[Prospeo.io](https://www.linkedin.com/company/getprospeo?trk=public_post-text), I didn’t think much of it.
Just another job application in a long list of tabs open on my laptop.
Resume, cover letter, recommendations, and send.
Then came the follow-up: a short pre-interview questionnaire about content creation, brand building, and growth tactics.
And at the very end, a small note:
"Please record a 1-2 minute video explaining how you’d create a content calendar."
I remember reading that and thinking, ugh, f\*\*k me, not a video.
I’d been grinding applications for months. Burnt out. Stressed. And for some reason, even though I’d been on camera before, the thought of recording myself felt exhausting.
But eventually, I said screw it.
I set up my camera, wrote a quick script, and asked my girlfriend to help film.
She stood behind the camera, holding my laptop like a teleprompter, laughing every few takes until we finally had something usable.
I edited it, added some b-roll, cleaned up the pacing, and treated it like a real piece of content.
Not just a quick Loom or one-take answer like most applicants sent in.
A few days later, I met with my now-CMO.
We talked for hours.
Content, growth, strategy. It just clicked.
The rest is history.
That one short video, the one I almost didn’t make, changed everything.
And that’s kind of the point.
It’s actually not that hard to stand out.
You don’t need to be the smartest or the most talented.
You just need to put in the extra effort most people aren’t willing to.
That mindset, doing a little more than what’s asked, has always been where my biggest opportunities came from.
Sometimes the thing that changes your life hides behind something small and inconvenient.
Something you really don’t feel like doing.
So if you’re tired, burnt out, and one click away from skipping that next task, don’t.
Film the video.
Write the post.
Take the shot.
It might be the moment that changes everything.
Curious to see the video that got me hired?

Senior AE @ Deel | I post about cold calling, sales & mindset
1moEdited
I get lots of DMs asking how to land a job at Deel.
So here’re my best tips.
(and honestly, these apply to any company in today’s competitive job market):
𝟭. 𝗗𝗼𝗻’𝘁𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗗𝗠𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗖𝗩:
If nobody at the company knows you, no one will put their name on the line to refer you. That’s just the truth.
𝟮. 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲’𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲:
Some ask for “30 minutes to pick your brain” out of the blue. Why would a stranger give you half an hour? Start smaller. Engage publicly. Build a relationship first.
𝟯. 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝗳𝗮𝗺𝗶𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆:
Follow employees. Comment on their posts. Share your own thoughts on the industry. If people see your name pop up consistently, you’ll stand out when you apply.
𝟰. 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗮𝗴𝗲𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁:
Employees post a lot about product updates, customer wins, sales tactics, etc. Drop meaningful comments, not just “great post.” Show you understand what the company does and care about it.
𝟱. 𝗖𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗲𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗼𝘄𝗻𝗳𝗼𝗼𝘁𝗽𝗿𝗶𝗻𝘁:
Post on LinkedIn. Talk about the industry, the problems the company solves, the trends shaping it. Show you already think like someone who works there.
𝟲. 𝗕𝗲𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗼𝘂𝘁𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗰𝗵:
A short personalized video to a hiring manager beats a cold “pls refer me” any day. Show why you’d be a fit, what excites you, and how you’d add value.
𝟳. 𝗔𝗽𝗽𝗹𝘆𝘀𝗺𝗮𝗿𝘁:
When you do apply, make sure your CV and cover letter are tailored. Generic apps get generic results.
Landing a role today isn’t about shortcuts. It’s about showing you understand the mission, fit the culture, and are serious enough to put in the work before asking for help.
Now here’s a question for you: Have you ever cold called a hiring manager?

1mo
🚀The Truth About Getting Hired in 2025
Getting a job today isn’t just about applying —it’s about standing out in a world full of talented people.
Here’s what most won’t tell you 👇1️⃣ Employers don’t always hire the best —they hire the clearest.
If your résumé and message don’t quickly explain why you’re the right fit, they’ll move on.
Make it simple, human, and focused on results —not buzzwords.
2️⃣ Connections beat applications.
Most jobs aren’t found on job boards —they’re found through people.
Start conversations.
Comment on posts.
Reach out with a genuine “I admire what you do, could I ask for a bit of advice?”
That’s how real opportunities start.
3️⃣ Prove it, don’t just promise it.
Anyone can say they’re hardworking or creative.
Show it through a small project, a portfolio, or an example of how you add value.
Proof speaks louder than adjectives.
4️⃣ Stay ready.
Keep your résumé fresh, your portfolio handy, and your 3-sentence self-intro sharp:
“I help [type of company] do [what you do] by [how you do it].”
5️⃣ Think like a marketer.
You’re not just job hunting —you’re positioning yourself as the solution to someone’s problem.
💡The truth: The people who get hired aren’t always the most qualified —they’re the most visible, prepared, and intentional.
If you’re job hunting right now —keep showing up, keep refining, and don’t underestimate the power of one good conversation.

1mo
𝐈𝐟𝐈𝐰𝐞𝐫𝐞𝐢𝐧𝐚𝐣𝐨𝐛𝐬𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡𝐭𝐨𝐝𝐚𝐲, 𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞’𝐬𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭𝐈’𝐝𝐝𝐨- 𝐚𝐧𝐝𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭𝐈𝐰𝐨𝐮𝐥𝐝𝐧’𝐭.
𝐖𝐡𝐚𝐭𝐈’𝐝𝐝𝐨:
▪️Build one resume and one profile that are authentic and represent who I actually am, not versions that chase job postings.
▪️Treat my LinkedIn profile like my personal brand site. I’d approach it like SEO: make it easy to understand what I do, where I add value, and what results I've driven. Use clear signals, keywords, and proof of work.
▪️Keep my content short, value-rich, and easy to scan. Focus on skills, experience, achievements, and metrics that speak for themselves.
▪️Leverage my network and my network’s network. Most real opportunities still start with people, not platforms.
▪️Stay proactive. The best roles usually aren’t sitting in a public posting.
𝐖𝐡𝐚𝐭𝐈𝐰𝐨𝐮𝐥𝐝𝐧’𝐭𝐝𝐨:
▪️Create multiple resumes to match every job description. Whatever you submit lives in an ATS and becomes your record. Inconsistencies can work against you later.
▪️Write long narratives or try to “storytell” my way into a role. Save that for the interview, where it can actually land.
▪️Assume recruiters or hiring managers will dig for the good stuff. If it’s buried, it’s invisible.
▪️Wait to be discovered or rely on algorithms. They’re not working in your favor.
Even the best candidates get overlooked when their story isn’t clear.

Posting 6 times a week is not a strategy.
It’s noise.
Because frequency without intention is like showing up to a job interview in pajamas 6 times and wondering why nobody hires you.
Here is the hard truth:
→More posts doesn’t mean more reach.
→More reach doesn’t mean more clients.
→And creating content just to “stay consistent” often leads to burnout, not business.
Ask yourself:
Would you rather have 100 likes from people who will never buy,
or 10 comments from decision-makers actively looking for what you sell?
High-value clients are not counting your posts.
They’re busy running companies, managing teams, solving real problems.
They’re not waiting for daily content drops.
They’re waiting for that one post that speaks to their exact problem at the exact right time.
That’s why posting less but with more focus, creates better results.
2–3 intentional posts a week outperform 6 generic ones every time.
Because the goal is not to go viral.
The goal is to be valuable.
So before hitting publish, ask:
Who is this for?
What problem does it solve?
What should they do next?
If you can’t answer clearly, don’t post it.
Spend that time creating something worth stopping for instead.

[Alejandro Spicker](https://de.linkedin.com/in/aspicker?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)
Head of Human Resources - exmox - We’re Hiring! | Global HR Executive | Transforming People Strategy, Leadership Development & DEI Across High-Growth Tech Environments
3w
Reaching out to me won’t get you the job (but this might).
I get so many messages every day on LinkedIn saying they’ve applied for a role at our company or that they’d like to apply.
While I appreciate the initiative, here’s the truth: Reaching out to me doesn’t really increase anyone’s chances of getting hired.
Not because I don’t care. I do. But our recruiting process is designed to give everyone a fair shot.
It starts with the submission of an application through the job posting. That’s where someone enters our system, where information is completed, and where at the end of the day, applicants own their own narrative.
If you want to use your time wisely, don’t spend it chasing me or anyone on LinkedIn. Spend it learning about the company.
Read up on our culture and our values.
Check out the hiring team.
Understand our business, challenges, pain points and what we’re actually looking for.
Then, show how you, your skills, mindset, and experience align with that.
Because at the end of the day, getting the job is only step one. You also need to be able to do the job, enjoy it, and grow with it.
Bottom line: A personalized message can open a door, sure, but deep understanding is what keeps it open.

I completely understand and agree with the fact that HR professionals really DO get flooded with "I just applied to X position" messages every day. It can feel a little bit like Harry Potter when his Hogwarts letters start flying out of the fireplace. 🤣🪄✉️That said, I do think there is a ton of real value in thoughtful outreach. A personal, intentional message that shows you have a genuine curiosity about the company, an open role, or even just a shared interest - now THAT's networking magic. So, I totally agree - we can't respond to every "I applied" message and it won't necessarily make your application more likely to get to the top of a recruiter's pile. But meaningful connections and conversations are what makes this platform powerful. One last note from me: beware the dark magic of autocorrect. Reaching out to me won’t get you the job (but this might).
I get so many messages every day on LinkedIn saying they’ve applied for a role at our company or that they’d like to apply.
While I appreciate the initiative, here’s the truth: Reaching out to me doesn’t really increase anyone’s chances of getting hired.
Not because I don’t care. I do. But our recruiting process is designed to give everyone a fair shot.
It starts with the submission of an application through the job posting. That’s where someone enters our system, where information is completed, and where at the end of the day, applicants own their own narrative.
If you want to use your time wisely, don’t spend it chasing me or anyone on LinkedIn. Spend it learning about the company.
Read up on our culture and our values.
Check out the hiring team.
Understand our business, challenges, pain points and what we’re actually looking for.
Then, show how you, your skills, mindset, and experience align with that.
Because at the end of the day, getting the job is only step one. You also need to be able to do the job, enjoy it, and grow with it.
Bottom line: A personalized message can open a door, sure, but deep understanding is what keeps it open.

I’m going to be quite direct here (who do I think I am?!)
It’s absolutely fine to speak to lots of people when hiring, especially for Resident positions. I mean after all, that’s the job! You want someone who is naturally personable, who brings energy, and who makes residents feel at home.
But here’s the catch by doing so:
🔹Reading through tons & tons of CVs - can be mundane and don’t show the warmth you need. 🔹Interviews!! if you’re meeting every single person who applies, it’s exhausting. And often, nerves get in the way of seeing someone’s true potential, nerves can mask that gut instinct. 🔹In BTR, Co-Living, and PBSA, it’s about how someone makes people feel, not just how they answer questions in a structured way.
That’s exactly why companies bring recruiters like me on board.
I speak to hundreds of people so you don’t have to.
I filter, I assess, I dig deeper.
And then I give you 3–4 people who are genuinely worth your time.
✅Personable
✅Presentable
✅Property trainable
✅Resident ready
Volume is fine. But don’t lose the standout who gave you that gut feeling while waiting for a unicorn.

If I had only 30 days to land a job, here’s exactly what I’d do:
Week 1: Set the Foundation
→Polish my resume (outcomes, not tasks)
→Optimize LinkedIn (headline, keywords, featured sec)
→Create a 2-line pitch: who I am + what I solve
→Make a list of 50 ex-colleagues, managers, vendors
Week 2: Build Momentum
→Reach out to those 50, not to beg, but to inform
→Start posting/commenting on LinkedIn daily
→Apply to 5–10 roles(meeting70% of the requirements)
→Create Notion tracker: job leads, outreach, follow-ups
Week 3: Practice + Position
→Mock interviews with friends or mentors
→Record answering “Tell me about yourself” and fix it
→Share 1 story on LinkedIn: a project, challenge/ insight
→Send 15 personalized DMs to hiring managers
Week 4: Focus and Follow-Up
→Reconnect with warm leads
→Reapply/ re-engage for any slow/no responses
→Add momentum: referrals, recruiters, niche job boards
→Rest, reflect, reset: job search is a marathon, (not a meltdown)
Remember: It’s not about luck. It’s about a system.

In his 17 years running[Airbnb](https://www.linkedin.com/company/airbnb?trk=public_post-text), CEO and cofounder[Brian Chesky](https://www.linkedin.com/in/brianchesky?trk=public_post-text)says he’s ignored popular leadership advice that he’s found imprudent. Within that category is the belief that leaders shouldn’t play favorites.
He concedes that the idea of heralding certain employees has a negative connotation. “At most companies, there’d be a protest if the CEO picked [favorites],” Chesky said in a recent interview with Fortune. “It would be considered unfair and not systematic.” But Chesky argues that when done well, employee favoritism allows leaders to spotlight top performers, tap their skill set and knowledge for strategy planning efforts, and demonstrate to other employees who they should emulate. Read more:[https://lnkd.in/edTrQtg7](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/edTrQtg7&urlhash=ZAxL&trk=public_post-text)

Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky rejects the assertion that high-performing organizations offer employees autonomy in spades.[https://lnkd.in/gHHt\_FzV](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/gHHt_FzV&urlhash=TtX5&trk=public_post-text)Chesky, just one of 22 Fortune 500 CEOs who can say they founded their empires, notes that his management philosophy isn’t an authoritarian approach to leadership, but it positions him as the ultimate decision-maker in an effort to streamline and expedite internal processes.
“I did all these things everyone told you to do, like hire great people, empower them to do their job, give them space, and the results were devastating,” Chesky says. 4mo
Are you a transformational or transactional leader?
Leadership styles can greatly influence your team’s performance and morale. There are two distinct leadership styles, transformational and transactional –let’s explore which style resonates with you.
Do you inspire change and empower your teams?
Transformational leaders drive change by motivating their teams with a compelling vision and fostering an environment of innovation. They focus on inspiring personal and professional growth, encouraging creativity, and building strong, collaborative cultures.
A great example of this style of leadership is Whitney Wolfe Herd, founder and CEO of Bumble, who epitomises transformational leadership. Wolfe Herd has built a company centred on empowering women, promoting inclusivity and creating meaningful social impact. Her leadership style is focused on motivating her team to contribute to a higher purpose, which has been instrumental in Bumble’s rapid growth. By encouraging a culture of innovation and inclusivity, Wolfe Herd has turned Bumble into a major player in the dating app industry.
Or do you drive results with structure and accountability?
Transactional leaders tend to focus on maintaining order and achieving specific, measurable results. They emphasise clear goals, performance metrics and rewards tied to achieving these goals. While they are often more focused on the short-term, this approach ensures efficiency and consistency, especially in fast-moving environments.
In the early stages of Airbnb’s growth, Brian Chesky, co-founder and CEO of Airbnb, demonstrated transactional leadership. Chesky’s focus on clear, actionable goals, such as expanding to new markets and increasing listings ensured that the company could scale quickly. His leadership was characterised by setting performance expectations and using rewards to incentivise employees to meet milestones, which allowed Airbnb to establish itself as a dominant force in the global hospitality industry.
Both transformational and transactional leadership styles have their merits. Transformational leaders thrive in environments where creativity and long-term vision are key, while transactional leaders excel when efficiency and clear targets are paramount.
The right style often depends on the stage and needs of the organisation.
Which leadership style defines you? 11moEdited
Anyone watched WeCrashed on AppleTV? Worth a watch if you haven’t! It charts the spectacular rise and fall of WeWork. I find it fascinating how collectively obsessed we are with ‘superstar' CEOs and how we idolise leaders who we deem irreplaceable. This feels particularly relevant at the moment with the headline grabbing stories about CEO succession at Disney and JP Morgan Chase. Isn’t a key part of the CEOs role to develop succession? And if so, why do we hold these ‘stars’ on such a pedestal? A friend recently reminded me of the work of James R. Meindl who talks about the "romance of leadership". This perspective suggests that we often attribute more influence and control to leaders than they realistically deserve. This fits our romantic (and let’s be honest, heavily gendered) notion of the lone hero who saves the day and wanders off in to the sunset
I've seen it too with many of my clients. Big organisational characters can make it harder for board members or succession candidates to conceive of any other type of leadership beyond the current incumbent. This can provoke an overly risk-led approach by the board and cause potential successors to play small, hence reinforcing any narrative of them not being ‘ready’. And while we’re at it, is anyone ever 100% ready? I don’t know the chap, but I imagine Jamie Dimon may have had to grow in to the role a little when he started. This risk can be mitigated by careful planning and by anchoring the needs of the new CEO against the strategy and context (rather than the incumbent). In addition, Nominations Committees can create opportunities for succession candidates to work with non-executives separately from the current CEO. This work must begin early and be sensitively managed to be effective and provide candidates with the chance to shine independently.
Are we too quick to attribute a company’s success to its CEO alone? Where does that leave the top team and wider leadership? How else can we prepare our organisations to thrive beyond a single iconic leader?[#succession](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/succession&trk=public_post-text)[#ceosuccession](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/ceosuccession&trk=public_post-text)[#successionplanning](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/successionplanning&trk=public_post-text)Thanks for the pointer[Dr. Aarti Shyamsunder (She/her)](https://in.linkedin.com/in/aartishyamsunder?trk=public_post-text)

Feeling stuck on which path to take as a leader?
A large majority of leaders (potentially around 75%) are confused about which leadership style to adopt.
Some have set their mind to adopt a collaborative and empowering style.
But seem to default to a more traditional and commanding approach.
So if you need some advice - be the leader who adapts, not the one who resists. Leaders who adapt quickly to market changes, customer needs, and emerging opportunities bring value by staying ahead.
The key to this leadership approach is agility.
Respond swiftly and effectively, turning challenges into opportunities.
- Keep up with news and trends
- Distribute leadership and encourage teams to take initiative
- Practice rotating strategies, reorganizing teams, and adjusting priorities for ease in response to uncertainties
Take this example from Airbnb during the COVID-19 pandemic:
Airbnb identified the new demand for remote locations and immediately shifted from long-term stays to short-term rentals.
They launched a new Online Experience future on their websites for users to engage in a variety of activities at home.
For Airbnb, the quarantine setup became an opportunity, which was once a threat to the hotel industry.
Time is money and the sooner you can adapt to changes, the more you're able to bounce back.[#leadership](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/leadership&trk=public_post-text)[#executiveadvice](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/executiveadvice&trk=public_post-text)[#executivecoaching](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/executivecoaching&trk=public_post-text)[#innovation](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/innovation&trk=public_post-text)[#businessgrowth](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/businessgrowth&trk=public_post-text)[#humanresources](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/humanresources&trk=public_post-text)[#success](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/success&trk=public_post-text)[#csuite](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/csuite&trk=public_post-text)[Mason & Co](https://sg.linkedin.com/company/mason-and-co?trk=public_post-text)

10mo
Being a CEO isn’t the same as it once was.
And the truth is, if businesses don’t adapt…
It could end up killing their results.
As the new generation enters the workplace, it’s becoming more apparent that:
→Old methods don’t work anymore.
→They have different needs & requirements.
→And they think very differently from how people did decades ago.
Yet, when it comes to how CEOs run their businesses… not much has changed.
And as a result, businesses are struggling to get the most out of their team.
So if CEOs want to thrive in 2024 and beyond, they are going to have to adapt and focus on:
→Supporting the team’s mindset.
→Adapting their leadership methods.
→Finding out what team members really want.
(The list could go on and on.)
But it boils down to building a mindset that allows you to be the best version of yourself.
↳If you can do that for you and your team… I guarantee you’ll see growth in the business.
It’s something that all the successful businesses are giving more attention to than ever before.
Otherwise, you’ll just be left behind by the businesses that do.

🚀Scaling from the Ground Up & Leading with Purpose 🚀Reflecting on the journey of scaling an operation from a small team to a thriving office in Maidstone, I’m reminded that growth isn’t just about increasing numbers but about building a strong foundation and leading with a vision.
Starting with just a handful of talented individuals, I focused on recruiting the right people, investing in coaching, and fostering a culture where each person could step into leadership. As we expanded into new regions and onboarded more brand ambassadors, the challenges grew, but so did the rewards.
What kept us grounded was a clear mission and relentless dedication:
•Empowering teams with the tools and autonomy they needed to succeed.
•Building trust by setting transparent goals and celebrating wins along the way.
•Scaling smartly by maintaining quality even as we grew quickly.
For anyone looking to scale, remember this: leading through growth means staying true to the core values that got you started while evolving your strategy to match your ambitions.
Here’s to building bigger, better, and bolder every day! 💪[#Leadership](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/leadership&trk=public_post-text)[#Growth](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/growth&trk=public_post-text)[#ScalingUp](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/scalingup&trk=public_post-text)[#Teamwork](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/teamwork&trk=public_post-text)[#BuildingSuccess](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/buildingsuccess&trk=public_post-text)

Is it possible for a company to thrive under a co-CEO model? A study by[Harvard Business Review](https://www.linkedin.com/company/harvard-business-review?trk=public_post-text)suggests that firms with co-CEOs might actually perform better, showing better returns than those headed by a single CEO. On the flip side, critics argue that as businesses scale, a singular visionary leadership style tends to be more effective. This topic has been explored in depth by James Cook for[Business Leader](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/business-leader-uk?trk=public_post-text), shedding light on both sides of the co-CEO debate. What are your thoughts on this? When COVID-19 upended the travel industry last year,[Airbnb](https://greylock.com/portfolio/#airbnb)was among its earliest victims, losing 80% of their business in the first two months of the pandemic. Such a blow would leave any CEO reeling, if not prove fatal to the company as a whole.
While many travel industry leaders chose to “go dark” while they decided how to navigate next steps, as[Airbnb CEO and co-founder Brian Chesky put it](https://news.airbnb.com/about-us/leadership/brian-chesky/), Chesky took a different approach: He got candid.
> “The biggest risk [for] the company is your stakeholders don’t trust you —not that you’ll say the wrong thing or get sued, but [that] people don’t trust you. [In a crisis], it’s really about being totally as transparent as you can be, trying to be incredibly compassionate, speaking from the heart.” Brian Chesky, CEO and co-founder of Airbnb
Chesky joined[Greylock partner Reid Hoffman](https://greylock.com/team/reid-hoffman/)(who[recently reflected](https://greylock.com/portfolio-news/airbnb-reflections/)on Airbnb’s 2010 Series A) for our[Iconversations speaker series](https://greylock.com/iconversations/)to talk about how he guided Airbnb from an 80% loss to a record-breaking IPO by the end of 2020, why Silicon Valley needs to redefine how it measures growth, how Chesky’s design background informs Airbnb’s business strategy and, ultimately, the importance of putting people first.
“Conventional wisdom says, ‘Don’t get too close, don’t get emotionally attached.’ Because if you do, it’s going to skew your decision making,” says Chesky. “I always saw it as the opposite.”

Of course, a good leader should have favorites and it should be based on fair criteria. As a founder and CEO, while I respect and value everyone on my team, I define clear and realistic success metrics as a measure for employee performance and productivity.
Remember, “It is an immutable law in business, that words are words, promises are promises, explanations are explanations, but only performance is reality”.
However, incentivizing high performers without making lower performers feel less of themselves will encouraging them to sit up and work harder.

In his 17 years running[Airbnb](https://www.linkedin.com/company/airbnb?trk=public_post_reshare-text), CEO and cofounder[Brian Chesky](https://www.linkedin.com/in/brianchesky?trk=public_post_reshare-text)says he’s ignored popular leadership advice that he’s found imprudent.[https://lnkd.in/edTrQtg7](https://lnkd.in/edTrQtg7?trk=public_post_reshare-text)Within that category is the belief that leaders shouldn’t play favorites. Chesky argues that when done well, employee favoritism allows leaders to spotlight top performers and demonstrate to other employees who they should emulate.
To be sure, so-called favorites should be selected based on fair criteria and concrete success metrics rather than sheer cronyism. Read more:[https://lnkd.in/edTrQtg7](https://lnkd.in/edTrQtg7?trk=public_post_reshare-text)

5moEdited
Is “favourites” the right word?
To me, it carries a hint of bias. While Chesky mentions a “fair criteria,” pairing “fair” with “favourite” doesn’t sit right. It risks fostering a cutthroat comparison culture—one that may drive short-term wins but ultimately erodes long-term performance.
In fact, Harvard Business School researchers Stein, Hobson, Jachimowicz, and Whillans report that environments driven by constant comparison reduce collaboration and innovation, leading to a 21% drop in employee engagement and company performance. Language matters, especially from the C-Suite.
I recommend using words that inspire individuals to achieve their own version of excellence—driven by support and growth, not by comparison. A great alternative could be “standouts”—a term that celebrates unique strengths and encourages everyone to excel in ways that are authentic to them.
By shifting the focus to personal growth and individual contributions, we create an environment where everyone is empowered to excel to the standards they are capable of without being made to feel “less than”.
What do you think? I just want to take a moment to talk about something that makes ALL the difference in your team—strong leadership.
Many leaders think they have to do it all themselves, but let me tell you… the real magic happens when you train others to lead. Your best employees? They don’t just want to follow. They want to grow, take on challenges, and become leaders themselves.
Here’s why it matters:
When you invest in leadership training, you’re saying, "I believe in you." And guess what? They feel that. They stay. They perform better. They bring that next-level energy.
What happens when you don’t?
You start losing your top talent because they don’t feel seen or supported. You end up back at square one, trying to replace them. Trust me, it’s way harder to replace a good leader than to grow one.
So, here’s the takeaway: Invest in your people.
Give them the tools to lead. Watch them step up and keep your team thriving.
And while we’re at it, let’s normalize celebrating this.
Celebrate when your employees take charge, celebrate when they crush their goals, and celebrate when they become the leaders you always knew they could be.

The article outlines crucial leadership lessons for turnarounds. Aligning the leadership team with the new strategy is vital, employing a "slow, slow, fast" approach: set the framework, analyze capabilities, and then act swiftly. Balancing the replacement of resistant leaders with retaining adaptable ones is essential, sometimes requiring external evaluations and coaching. Transparent conversations can convert resistant executives into allies. Avoid over-reliance on former colleagues to prevent favoritism perceptions. Strategic talent management involves recognizing operational and innovative capabilities. Clear board communication and alignment on expectations are crucial for driving successful transformation without destabilizing the organization.

6mo
UNIFIED LEADERSHIP IN FAMILY BUSINESSES
One of the biggest challenges for employees working in family businesses in the transition from founder era to generation era is dealing with two different leadership styles simultaneously. The founder’s approach may be deeply hands-on and traditional, while the next generation may bring a more modern way of running things. If employees are forced to navigate between two conflicting sets of directions, it can lead to frustration, confusion, and ultimately, disengagement. No one enjoys working in an environment where the goals are unclear, or where the leadership isn't aligned.
It’s important for the new generation of leaders to collaborate closely with the founder during the transition. A unified front is essential for maintaining morale and ensuring that the workforce remains engaged and motivated. Employees need to feel that they are working towards a common goal, under clear and cohesive leadership. Mixed signals only lead to half-hearted efforts and a lack of trust in management.

Hi Brian, I just listened to the update on Airbnb’s growth — congratulations, it’s impressive to see. I’ve been a host for over six years, and while I truly appreciate how AI helps with translations and suggested messages, hosting is still a very human experience. We deal with unexpected and complex situations all the time. I’ve sometimes reached support and felt like the person on the phone had just woken up. More importantly, it often feels like they don’t fully understand how complex hosting really is. We’re not only managing what happens inside a reservation — we deal with city electricity outages, water issues, building problems, and situations completely outside our control. Being a host is very different from being a guest. The level of responsibility and risk is not the same. While AI gives us support on many levels, from a host perspective it feels like the quality of customer support is decreasing, especially when real and complex issues arise. Airbnb has changed my life, and I share this because I truly care about its future. Best, Mariana

While margins and EBITDA are improving, support quality is a critical long-term asset. If hosts feel unsupported, the platform risks weakening its most important foundation: the hosts who generate the bookings and commissions.

Brian Chesky's "Don't push decision making down" approach is brilliant

A little over a week back I was watching a recent interview of Brian Chesky.
And he said something very counterintuitive to most folks.
You can even say its probably an unpopular thing to say.
But it was something that I totally totally agree with.
He said he doesn’t aim to empower all the various departments of AirBnB to take their own decisions.
> Rather he tries to centralize most core decisions to himself. Or at least to how he would have decided.
Now let’s dissect this a bit and I’ll point out why I love it.

I’ve been a part of a number of companies scaling at various stages. Companies like Groupon, Lazada, Wayfair and Pomelo Fashion.
And what you tend to see as they get bigger is that you get more levels in the organization.
And so a hierarchy is set up in which the CEO manages a handful (usually max 7) direct reports. Then each of those C-levels in turn manage a set of direct reports. And on and on till you get the final front line-level employees.
Then there is usually some type of annual, or sometimes quarterly, strategic planning process to set high level goals (or in some companies they use OKR’s).
And each group in each level of the organization runs at implementing their OKR’s. And reports back on them at the end of each quarter usually.
> Now this is a beautiful model if we lived in a static world where nothing is changing. But that is never the reality.
The reality is that tons of things are changing all of the time.

Teams do not live in isolation. Marketing is often tied to supply chain, which is tied to other functions.
A change in one has a ripple effect on the others.
So for example if you’re not able to produce the hot new product you planned… then perhaps this nullifies part of your marketing strategy.
And you hope that your interdepartmental communication is good enough to fully think this out. Which sometimes it is, but sometimes it isn’t.
> What often gets missed is the impacts on other teams that are less direct. For example, maybe you should also adjust your pricing and your distribution approach.
Then maybe when you change them, you don’t even know what you’re changing them to. So you need to test and iterate.
But how do you do that when you’re driving an unwieldy ship of several thousand employees.
For example AirBnB is now 7000+ employees. It is like the Titanic.

What you start to see is teams throughout the organization starting to make their own decisions on how to adjust their strategy.
In part because they are ‘empowered’ to do so.
> The problem is that the folks in these teams are often not talking to the other departments or thinking about the full impact of their decisions.
Sometimes they’re not even capable to. Or they don’t see that as being part of their job.
And so it ends up not working. Or only partially working.
As a result resources are wasted.
Projects fail.
I’ve seen it happen a ton of times.

What Smart CEOs Know: Scaling GTM Can Kill You If… Charlie Latch 9 months ago
Speed Wins: The GTM Advantage Most CEOs Waste Charlie Latch 10 months ago
Purpose, Change and Aristotle Jorge García-Luengo 10 years ago
Startups are fast and nimble

The type of problem I’ve described above typically doesn’t happen when the team is small and is still a startup of say less than 50 people.
Why? Because everyone is in close proximity to one another and they communicate.
> They can test and iterate quickly because of their small scale. So they have a much higher chance of landing on the right path or solution.
It’s kind of like seeing an iceberg ahead in your path… and you’re driving a speedboat instead of the titanic.
There’s a much better shot of you switching direction in time.
Can your startup be fast and nimble... while still being big?

This was the problem that Brian Chesky was faced with. Because he wanted AirBnB to not become the Titanic but rather still operate like a speedboat and make big changes fast and successfully.
So what did he do? He centralized decision-making to himself as much as he could.
> Or as he says it in the interview… “my job as a leader is to flatten the org.”
This doesn’t mean that he took every decision himself. But rather he’d been involved directly with enough people at various levels of the organization that even if he wasn’t directly in the meeting… they knew how Brian would think about it.
So how did he do this practically? Well he set up the organization such that the teams implementing the key strategic projects had direct exposure to him.
This way they would get direct access to how he thinks and makes decisions.
And after awhile.. even if he wasn’t in the room they’d be trained to have the long-term, customer experience-first way of thinking that he did.
He didn’t rely on the leader of an important team passing that culture and mindset to their team indirectly… because that would be like playing the kids game telephone.
He exposed multiple layers of the org to his way of thinking and decision-making directly.
And I honestly think that that is genius.
What if your Titanic is fast and nimble, and there are clear beacons that guide the way?

As many folks know I’m a Clickup guy.
Clickup is a project management application but the way that I use it…. it is much much more than that.
I’ve set up two of my own startups and ran it completely on Clickup with the mindset of “everything that takes more than twenty minutes is a Clickup task."
I’ve also set up several funded startups that were clients and larger client organisations
.Clickup is in my view amazing at two things…. setting up clear, transparent processes. And the ability to structure your entire organization into a transparent way of working.
> With the Clickup approach I use…. I easily have transparency into the decisions and progress being made by 40+ people. I could probably even do 100+ if I wanted.
Because you’re just reading through and occasionally responding to notifications and comments.
So influencing managers’ decision-making two or even three levels down the org chart all of a sudden becomes possible to do systematically.
Once leaders of larger organizations begin to fully understand just how powerful this is I think it is going to be a game changer.
Because you can run a large organization with hundreds or even thousands of people almost like a startup.
Which means you react and iterate light years faster than traditional organisations of that size. Which essentially means that in the end… you win.

Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky got heated as we talked cleaning fees in his company’s New York City offices yesterday. We were debating whether Airbnb should display total price per night by default, and Chesky jumped into a breakdown of hospitality industry products and practices before precisely explaining how Airbnb fit and its tradeoffs. “I actually stay in the details of the product,” he told me. “CEOs at S&P 500, Fortune 100 companies, I think most of them aren't really in the details of their product. At some point, these companies get so big, that it's pretty typical that you get a little detached. That it's someone else's job to listen to customers. But it's not your job. And I've always wanted to be the kind of company that felt more like a startup.” After admitting he was once a bit too removed from customers, Chesky is clearly all in on product now. During the conversation, now live on Big Technology Podcast, he announced the results of Twitter (or X) survey he posted, asking customers for feedback on the product. Their suggestions about cleaning fees, listing reliability, and customer services led to real product results. Some big changes included more than 260,000 Airbnb properties lowering or removing cleaning fees, verified listings spinning up in five countries, and pet friendly listings — and even king beds — getting added to search. Autonomy and removed leadership is kind of in vogue in the tech world right now, but Chesky is putting himself in the middle of the process. Consider: 👉 He’s turned product management into product marketing as he and his executive team have gotten more involved in building. 👉 Airbnb runs an Apple-style development process, putting hardware lessons into play at a software company, and shipping big updates twice per year. 👉 Chesky says the company’s shipped 250-300 new updates since moving to a new model where he’s been more involved. “The velocity is somewhere between 2 and 5x greater than it was before,” he said. “The quality of the product is better.” Do you think Chesky’s approach is working? Is it good for CEOs to be this engaged? From my vantage point, Chesky's interaction with customers is really notable, and will pay off in the long run. And in the short term, I’d still recommend a cap on cleaning fees, or perhaps an outright ban. Give the podcast a listen, share with a friend, and let me hear your feedback the comments!
